cases.title: Bradycardia in a Toddler
cases.presentation: A 3-year-old with a severe asthma attack becomes unresponsive. You are ventilating effectively with a bag-mask and good chest rise, but the heart rate is 48/min and the child is mottled and limp.
cases.question: What is the correct action?
options: A: Continue only ventilation and reassess in 5 minutes.
B: Begin chest compressions because the heart rate is under 60 with poor perfusion despite ventilation.
C: Apply an AED and shock the bradycardia.
cases.explanation: A child with a heart rate below 60/min and signs of poor perfusion despite adequate oxygenation and ventilation should receive chest compressions. A slow, poorly perfusing rhythm in a child quickly deteriorates to full arrest.
cases.clinicalPearl: In children, 'under 60 with poor perfusion' is a compression trigger — you don't wait for the pulse to disappear.

cases.title: Two Rescuers, One Child
cases.presentation: A 7-year-old is in cardiac arrest after a drowning. You and a colleague are both trained rescuers with a bag-mask and AED available.
cases.question: What compression-to-ventilation ratio and technique should you use?
options: B: 15:2, one or two hands to a depth of at least one-third the chest, rate 100–120/min, with breaths.
cases.explanation: With two rescuers, child CPR is 15:2. Compress the lower half of the sternum with one or two hands to at least one-third the chest depth at 100–120/min, including ventilations — especially important here because drowning is a hypoxic, breathing-driven arrest.
cases.clinicalPearl: Two rescuers + child = 15:2, and never skip breaths in a drowning arrest.

cases.title: Alone With a Collapsed Child
cases.presentation: You are alone and find a 5-year-old unresponsive on the floor; you did not see the collapse and no one else is around. The child is not breathing and has no pulse. You have a phone.
cases.question: What is the best sequence for a lone rescuer?
options: A: Leave to find an AED across the building before doing anything.
B: Give about 2 minutes of CPR first (likely hypoxic), then activate the emergency response and get an AED — or call immediately on speaker while starting CPR.
C: Only give breaths and wait.
D: Apply an AED before any CPR.
cases.explanation: For an unwitnessed pediatric collapse with a lone rescuer, the likely cause is hypoxic, so about 2 minutes of CPR is provided before leaving to activate help and get an AED. Using a phone on speaker lets the rescuer call without interrupting CPR.
cases.clinicalPearl: Unwitnessed child collapse alone = CPR first; a speakerphone lets you do both at once.
